sentence: Subjective:
The patient is a 54-year-old male presenting with a 2-3 month history of stabbing, shooting pain in the right cheekbone area, which is exacerbated by cold temperatures and wind.
The pain is described as excruciating, lasting from a few minutes to an hour, and is not accompanied by numbness, tingling, or weakness on the right side of the face.
The patient has tried ibuprofen, Motrin, and tiger balm with minimal relief.
He has a history of occasional headaches, but no history of migraines or brain tumors.
Past medical history:
The patient has a past medical history of occasional headaches, but no significant past medical conditions, treatments, or encounters are mentioned.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any sensory loss, numbness, or tingling on the left side of the face.
He also denies any weakness on the right side of the face, including when smiling or performing other facial movements.
Current medications:
The patient is currently taking Tylenol for occasional headaches.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam:
The patient has symmetric teeth and can follow the doctor's finger in both directions.
He can hear sounds on both sides of his face, and can feel the doctor's cotton tape on the left side of his face, but reports numbness on the right side.
The patient feels a dull sensation on the right side when the doctor pokes with a needle, and a sharp sensation on the left side.
Test Results:
The patient's MRI shows a small blood vessel abutting and perhaps pinching the trigeminal nerve.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has trigeminal neuralgia, a condition where nerve compression causes shooting electrical pain in the face.
Problem list: Trigeminal neuralgia; confirmed.
Plan:
I prescribed Tegretol 100mg orally once daily for the treatment of trigeminal neuralgia.
I explained the potential side effects of the medication, including memory loss, tingling, imbalance, and dermatologic reactions.
The patient agreed to undergo genetic testing to ensure the medication is safe for him.
We will follow up in one month to assess the effectiveness of the medication and discuss further treatment options if necessary.
Follow-up:
The patient will return in one month to reassess the effectiveness of the medication and discuss further treatment options if necessary.
